file_name: Tziomaka and Maglogiannis - 2021 - Ensembles of deep convolutional neural networks for detecting melanoma in dermoscopy images.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning, specifically ensembles of EfficientNet (a family of deep convolutional neural networks), to perform binary classification of malignant melanoma from dermoscopic images. The methodology includes fine-tuning pretrained CNNs, loss balancing, and model ensembling to detect melanoma in the SIIM-ISIC dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work for the SIIM-ISIC Melanoma Classification Challenge. It describes a novel methodology utilizing ensembles of EfficientNet CNNs, loss balancing, and metadata integration on the ISIC2019 and ISIC2020 datasets. The structure includes specific sections for Materials and Methods, Experiments and Results (with quantitative metrics like AUC scores), and Discussion, indicating it is an original technical study rather than a review.

title: Ensembles of Deep Convolutional Neural Networks for Detecting Melanoma in Dermoscopy Images
brief_summary: This study addresses the binary classification of malignant melanoma from dermoscopic images for the SIIM-ISIC Melanoma Classification Challenge. The authors utilized the EfficientNet family of deep convolutional neural networks, fine-tuned on a combined dataset of 58,457 images from the ISIC2019 and ISIC2020 archives. To handle severe class imbalance and varying image resolutions, the methodology incorporated loss balancing and multi-resolution model inputs. Additionally, an ensembling strategy was applied to models trained with different activation functions to enhance diversity and performance. The approach aimed to improve detection accuracy by leveraging metadata and advanced deep learning techniques on the large-scale ISIC dataset.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for the binary classification task. The paper focuses on detecting malignant melanoma. In the combined dataset (ISIC2019 + ISIC2020), there were 5,106 melanoma instances.

The negative class in the binary classification task. In the combined dataset, there were 53,351 non-melanoma instances. The ISIC2020 dataset specifically included benign lesions of types: nevus, seborrheic keratosis, lichenoid keratosis, solar lentigo, lentigo NOS, cafe-au-lait macule, atypical melanocytic proliferation, and unknown.

all_task_types.details: The paper addresses the SIIM-ISIC Melanoma Classification Challenge, which is a binary classification problem. The goal is to detect malignant melanoma from dermoscopic images, predicting one of two discrete classes: melanoma or non-melanoma. The evaluation metric used is the Area Under the Curve (AUC) score.

datasets_used.name: ISIC2019 Challenge Dataset
datasets_used.description: Part of the ISIC archive, titled 'Skin Lesion Analysis Towards Melanoma Detection'. It contains labeled dermoscopy images and metadata (site, age, gender). Diagnoses include melanoma, melanocytic nevus, basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis, benign keratosis, dermatofibroma, vascular lesion, and squamous cell carcinoma.

use.training: Combined with ISIC2020 to form the training dataset for the binary classification task.

datasets_used.name: ISIC2020 Challenge Dataset
datasets_used.description: Part of the ISIC archive, titled 'Skin Lesion Analysis Towards Melanoma Detection'. It contains benign and malignant skin lesions from over 2,000 patients with metadata (diagnosis, site, age, gender, patient ID). Benign images are of 8 types (nevus, seborrheic keratosis, lichenoid keratosis, solar lentigo, lentigo NOS, cafe-au-lait macule, atypical melanocytic proliferation, unknown). Malignant images are all melanoma. No basal cell or squamous cell carcinoma cases are present.

use.training: Combined with ISIC2019 to form the training dataset for the binary classification task.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Unlabeled Evaluation Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset of unlabeled images with metadata (except diagnosis) provided by ISIC for algorithm evaluation. The evaluation is automated by ISIC, providing ROC-AUC scores.

use.testing: Used for automated evaluation by ISIC to provide ROC-AUC scores for submissions.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To address class imbalance by computing class weights from normalized inverse class frequencies in the training set.
To evaluate and compare model performance on the validation set (for model selection) and the test set/unlabeled evaluation dataset.
To combine predictions from individual models to improve performance and robustness.
To split the dataset into training, validation, and test subsets for model development and evaluation.
To fine-tune all layers of the CNNs during training.
To manage the learning rate during training, starting according to Leslie Smith's 2017 paper and applying a 0.97 decay ratio per epoch.
To calculate feature attributions and visualize explainability for the EfficientNet-B3–B6 models.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using ROC-AUC scores on the validation set and the unlabeled evaluation dataset.

ISIC2019 + ISIC2020 Combined Dataset.description: Combined training dataset of 58,457 images (5,106 melanoma, 53,351 non-melanoma). Partitioned into 80% training, 10% validation, 10% test.
ISIC2019 + ISIC2020 Combined Dataset.notes: The 'Ensemble Optimal' model, consisting of EfficientNet-B4–B5 and EfficientNet-B3–B6 with Mish activation and metadata, achieved the highest test set AUC of 0.9810.

ISIC Unlabeled Evaluation Dataset.description: 10,982 unlabeled images with metadata (except diagnosis). Evaluation provided by ISIC automated system.
ISIC Unlabeled Evaluation Dataset.notes: The best model achieved a 0.9404 AUC score on the automatic evaluation system, placing it among the top-5% performances. The optimal ensemble consisted of EfficientNet-B4–B5 and EfficientNet-B3–B6 with Mish activation and metadata.

architecture_used.input_size: Variable based on model variant: B3 (300x300), B4 (380x380), B5 (456x456), B6 (528x528)

architecture_details.backbone: EfficientNet with Mobile Inverted Bottleneck Conv (MBconv) Blocks and depth-wise separable convolution
architecture_details.compound_scaling: Follows compound scaling rule for adjustment to larger image sizes

metadata_integration.description: Modified architecture incorporating patient metadata (age, biological sex, anatomy site, number of images, image size)
metadata_integration.layers: 3 additional layers: one taking input from metadata and default EfficientNet output, two lower layers with Batch Normalization and Dropout (30% chance)

training_details.loss_function: Weighted cross-entropy loss (weights computed by normalizing inverse class frequencies)
training_details.learning_rate_schedule: Cyclical learning rates (based on Leslie Smith 2017) with 0.97 decay ratio per epoch
training_details.initialization: ImageNet pretrained weights for backbone; Uniform LeCun method for additional layers in modified architecture

classification_or_output_head.modification: Original 1000-dim ImageNet softmax layer replaced with 2-neuron softmax layer

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: EfficientNet-B3 with Mish activation and metadata

Fully convolutional residual networks (mentioned in related work)
preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to specific model input dimensions (EfficientNet-B3: 300x300, B4: 380x380, B5: 456x456, B6: 528x528)
Normalization by subtracting ImageNet mean and dividing by ImageNet standard deviation RGB values
Data augmentation: horizontal flip, vertical flip, color jitter, and random erasing

conclusions: An ensemble method combining deep neural networks of varying scales (EfficientNet-B3 to B6) and trained with different activation functions (Swish and Mish) achieves competitive classification performance for detecting melanoma in dermoscopic images.

The optimal ensemble model, which includes EfficientNet-B4–B5 and EfficientNet-B3–B6 with Mish activation and metadata integration, achieved a ROC-AUC score of 0.9404 on the SIIM-ISIC public leaderboard, placing it among the top-5% performances.
The optimal ensemble achieved a test set ROC-AUC score of 0.9810 on the combined ISIC2019 and ISIC2020 dataset.

Incorporating patient metadata (age, biological sex, anatomy site, etc.) into the model architecture alongside a different activation function (Mish) further diversifies the ensemble and improves performance compared to ensembles of just varying scale networks.
Loss balancing (weighted cross-entropy) and heavy data augmentation were effective strategies for addressing the severe class imbalance and avoiding overfitting.
Using multi-resolution inputs (varying image sizes) was necessary to handle the dataset's heterogeneous image resolutions.

Deeper and more complex networks with larger input resolutions did not provide significantly greater results compared to smaller variants, suggesting that ensemble diversity and metadata integration were more critical drivers of performance than network depth alone.

Integrated gradients visualizations indicate that the models primarily focus on the edges of skin lesions (which are often uneven or notched in melanoma) and, to a lesser extent, the lesion itself and surrounding skin.

Ensembles generalize better to unseen data than single models because the diversity in network scales, resolutions, and activation functions allows different models to capture different features, with the ensemble considering the confidence levels of these varying models.
important_remarks: The study addresses a severe class imbalance (5,106 melanoma vs. 53,351 non-melanoma instances) by using weighted cross-entropy loss and heavy data augmentation, which are critical methodological choices for this dataset.

The dataset combines ISIC2019 and ISIC2020; notably, ISIC2020 lacks basal cell carcinoma and squamous cell carcinoma cases, meaning the model is optimized for melanoma vs. non-melanoma rather than multi-class skin cancer differentiation.
Evaluation relies on the SIIM-ISIC public leaderboard's automated ROC-AUC score on an unlabeled test set (10,982 images), which limits independent statistical verification of the reported 0.9404 AUC.

The 'Ensemble Optimal' model achieved a test set AUC of 0.9810 on the combined training/validation/test split, but this internal test set is derived from the same source distribution as the training data, potentially inflating performance estimates compared to external clinical settings.
The paper does not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals for the performance metrics, relying solely on point estimates of AUC.

The models were trained on dermoscopic images with varying resolutions, addressed by using multi-resolution inputs (300x300 to 528x528), which is a strength for handling heterogeneous clinical data but increases computational complexity.

Metadata (age, gender, site) was integrated into the model architecture, which may introduce bias if the metadata distribution in future deployment settings differs from the ISIC archive demographics.

The study is a conference paper submission for a specific challenge, which may limit the depth of discussion regarding long-term clinical deployment, generalizability to non-ISIC datasets, or computational efficiency for real-time use.
